title: Subtracting fractions with unlike denominators
description: Practice subtracting fractions that have different denominators.
topic_path: Adding and subtracting fractions with unlike denominators

hints:
Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.


[[☃ image 1]]

Let's look at the multiples of each denominator and see which multiples they have in common.

Denominator | Multiples
:-: | :-:
$\greenD{2}$ | $2, {4}, \underline{6}$
$\purpleD{3}$ | $3, \underline{6}, 9$

The least common denominator is ${6}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $6$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{6}{2}}=\dfrac{\greenD{6} \times 3}{\greenD{2} \times 3} = {\dfrac\greenD{18}\greenD{6}}$

$\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}}=\dfrac{\purpleD{1} \times 2}{\purpleD{3} \times 2} = {\dfrac\purpleD{2}\purpleD{6}}$

$\greenD{\dfrac{6}{2}} - \purpleD{\dfrac{1}{3}} = \dfrac{16}{6}$

We can also write $\dfrac{16}{6}$ as $\dfrac83$ or $2\dfrac23$.

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's look at the multiples of each denominator and see which multiples they have in common.

Denominator | Multiples
:-: | :-:
$\greenD{3}$ | $3, {6}, 9, \underline{12}$
$\purpleD{4}$ | $4, 8, \underline{12}, 16$

The least common denominator is ${12}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $12$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{8}{3}}=\dfrac{\greenD{8} \times 4}{\greenD{3} \times 4} = {\dfrac\greenD{32}\greenD{12}}$

$\purpleD{\dfrac{6}{4}}=\dfrac{\purpleD{6} \times 3}{\purpleD{4} \times 3} = {\dfrac\purpleD{18}\purpleD{12}}$

$\greenD{\dfrac{8}{3}} - \purpleD{\dfrac{6}{4}} = \dfrac{14}{12}$

We can also write $\dfrac{14}{12}$ as $\dfrac76$ or $1\dfrac16$.

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.


[[☃ image 1]]

Let's look at the multiples of each denominator and see which multiples they have in common.

Denominator | Multiples
:-: | :-:
$\greenD{2}$ | $2, {4}, \underline{6}$
$\purpleD{6}$ | $\underline{6}, 12, 18$

The least common denominator is ${6}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $6$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{7}{2}}=\dfrac{\greenD{7} \times 3}{\greenD{2} \times 3} = {\dfrac\greenD{21}\greenD{6}}$

$\greenD{\dfrac{7}{2}} - \purpleD{\dfrac{7}{6}} = \dfrac{14}{6}$

We can also write $\dfrac{14}{6}$ as $\dfrac73$ or $2\dfrac13$.

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's look at the multiples of each denominator and see which multiples they have in common.

Denominator | Multiples

The least common denominator is ${8}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $8$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{5}{2}}=\dfrac{\greenD{5} \times 4}{\greenD{2} \times 4} = {\dfrac\greenD{20}\greenD{8}}$

$\greenD{\dfrac{5}{2}} - \purpleD{\dfrac{6}{8}} = \dfrac{14}{8}$

We can also write $\dfrac{14}{8}$ as $\dfrac74$ or $1\dfrac34$.

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's look at the multiples of each denominator and see which multiples they have in common.

Denominator | Multiples
:-: | :-:
$\greenD{2}$ | $2, {4}, 6, 8, 10, \underline{12}$
$\purpleD{12}$ | $\underline{12}, 24, 36$

The least common denominator is ${12}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $12$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{8}{2}}=\dfrac{\greenD{8} \times 6}{\greenD{2} \times 6} = {\dfrac\greenD{48}\greenD{12}}$

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's look at the multiples of each denominator and see which multiples they have in common.

The least common denominator is ${15}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $15$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{8}{3}}=\dfrac{\greenD{8} \times 5}{\greenD{3} \times 5} = {\dfrac\greenD{40}\greenD{15}}$

$\purpleD{\dfrac{1}{5}}=\dfrac{\purpleD{1} \times 3}{\purpleD{5} \times 3} = {\dfrac\purpleD{3}\purpleD{15}}$

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

[[☃ image 1]]

Let's look at the multiples of each denominator and see which multiples they have in common.

Denominator | Multiples
:-: | :-:
$\greenD{3}$ | $3,  \underline{6}, 9$
$\purpleD{6}$ | $\underline{6}, 12, 18$

The least common denominator is ${6}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $6$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{8}{3}}=\dfrac{\greenD{8} \times 2}{\greenD{3} \times 2} = {\dfrac\greenD{16}\greenD{6}}$

$\greenD{\dfrac{8}{3}} - \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{6}} = \dfrac{14}{6}$

We can also write $\dfrac{14}{6}$ as $\dfrac{7}{3}$ or $2\dfrac13$.

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.


[[☃ image 1]]

Let's look at the multiples of each denominator and see which multiples they have in common.

:-: | :-:
$\greenD{3}$ | $3,  {6}, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, \underline{24}$
$\purpleD{8}$ | $8, 16, \underline{24}, 32$

The least common denominator is ${24}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $24$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{8}{3}}=\dfrac{\greenD{8} \times 8}{\greenD{3} \times 8} = {\dfrac\greenD{64}\greenD{24}}$

$\purpleD{\dfrac{2}{8}}=\dfrac{\purpleD{2} \times 3}{\purpleD{8} \times 3} = {\dfrac\purpleD{6}\purpleD{24}}$

$\greenD{\dfrac{8}{3}} - \purpleD{\dfrac{2}{8}} = \dfrac{58}{24}$

We can also write $\dfrac{58}{24}$ as $\dfrac{29}{12}$ or $2\dfrac{5}{12}$.

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

$\greenD{3}$ | $3,  {6}, 9, 12, 15, 18, 21, 24, 27, \underline{30}$
$\purpleD{10}$ | $10,20, \underline{30}, 40$

The least common denominator is ${30}$.

Let's use multiplication to make each fraction have a denominator of $30$.

$\greenD{\dfrac{9}{3}}=\dfrac{\greenD{9} \times {10}}{\greenD{3} \times {10}} = {\dfrac\greenD{90}\greenD{30}}$

$\purpleD{\dfrac{6}{10}}=\dfrac{\purpleD{6} \times 3}{\purpleD{10} \times 3} = {\dfrac\purpleD{18}\purpleD{30}}$

$\greenD{\dfrac{9}{3}} - \purpleD{\dfrac{6}{10}} = \dfrac{72}{30}$

We can also write $\dfrac{72}{30}$ as $\dfrac{12}{5}$ or $2\dfrac{2}{5}$.

Before we can subtract our fractions, they need to have the same denominator.

[[☃ image 1]]
